content.text: Sandbox mode

lets you run a goal end-to-end — with real connector reads — without executing any write or send actions. Drive docs are shown as previews but not created. Slack messages are composed but not posted. Emails are drafted but not sent. The engine reasons with real data; only the output actions are held back.

This makes sandbox the right mode for testing new goals, validating skill templates before deploying to a workspace, training new users, and auditing what a scheduled goal will actually do before enabling it on a recurring schedule.

Sandbox is not a simulation of the connector data — it reads your real Gmail, Drive, and Slack. Only the write-side operations are intercepted. This means sandbox results reflect real data and are a reliable preview of what the live goal will produce.
What Sandbox Intercepts
Sandbox mode intercepts all actions that are difficult or impossible to reverse, while allowing all read operations to run normally so the engine has real data to reason from.
Intercepted (write/send/delete) actions
Creating a new Google Doc or Sheet — shows as a preview with the document content, but the file is not created in Drive
Writing to or appending rows in an existing Sheet — shows the proposed changes but does not modify the file
Posting a message to a Slack channel or thread — shows the message content but does not post
Sending a direct message in Slack — shows the draft but does not send
Sending or drafting an email in Gmail — shows the draft content but does not save to Drafts or send
Applying Gmail labels or archiving threads — shows which threads would be labelled but does not apply the labels
Any file delete or archive operation — logged in the sandbox report but not executed
Not intercepted (read) actions
Reading email threads from Gmail
Reading Google Doc or Sheet content
Searching Drive for files
Reading Slack channel messages and search results
Loading web pages via the Chrome connector
Reading PagerDuty incidents or other custom connector data sources

Because reads are not intercepted, sandbox mode gives you an accurate preview of what the live goal will produce. If the read data changes between the sandbox run and the live run, the live output may differ — but the sandbox output reflects what the engine saw at sandbox run time.
Enabling Sandbox Mode
Sandbox mode can be enabled at three levels: for a single goal run, as a default for your personal goals, or as a workspace-wide default (set by admins).
Per-goal sandbox

Toggle the Sandbox switch in the goal panel before running. The goal runs in sandbox mode for that invocation only — subsequent runs (including scheduled runs) default to the workspace or personal setting.
Open or write a goal
Click the Sandbox toggle in the run options panel (next to the Run button)
The toggle turns orange and shows "Sandbox" — the goal is now in sandbox mode for this run
Run the goal
Review the sandbox report (see below)
Toggle off and run again to execute in live mode
Personal sandbox default

Set sandbox as your personal default in Settings → Goals → Default to Sandbox. With this enabled, every goal you create starts in sandbox mode. You toggle to live mode explicitly when you are ready to execute for real.
This is the recommended setting for users who are new to Cowork or who frequently author new goals. It prevents accidental writes and sends during the authoring and testing phase.
Workspace sandbox default

Workspace admins can set sandbox as the default for all users in Settings → Workspace → Goal defaults → Default to Sandbox. When this is on, all goals in the workspace start in sandbox mode unless a user explicitly toggles to live.
Workspace sandbox default is appropriate for teams in the onboarding phase, where most users are still learning what goals do
It is also useful for compliance-heavy environments where every goal output should be reviewed before being written to external systems
Individual users can still override to live mode for their own goals if they have the permission
Scheduled goals ignore the workspace default and use the mode set explicitly on the goal — a scheduled goal set to live runs live even if the workspace default is sandbox
Reading the Sandbox Report

After a sandbox run, Cowork presents a sandbox report instead of the normal goal completion view. The report shows what the engine reasoned through, what it would have written or sent, and a list of all intercepted actions.
Report sections
Section
Contents
Goal reasoning trace
The engine's step-by-step reasoning — what it read, how it interpreted the data, what it planned to do
Intercepted write actions
Each write/send/post action that was blocked, with the full proposed content shown inline
Read connector activity
Summary of what the engine read: which files, which threads, which pages, and the data it extracted
Proposed output (inline)
The final output as it would appear — doc content, Slack message text, email body — formatted for review
Action log
Timestamped list of every connector call, both reads and blocked writes, in execution order
Reviewing a Drive write in the sandbox report

When a goal would create a Drive document, the sandbox report shows the full document content inline as a preview. If you are testing a deal memo skill, you will see the formatted memo exactly as it would appear in Drive — headings, bullet points, tables, and all. You can copy the preview text or send it to a reviewer before running the goal live.
Reviewing a Slack post in the sandbox report

Blocked Slack posts appear in the sandbox report with the full message text and the channel it would have been posted to. If the message looks correct, run the goal live. If you want to adjust the message, edit the goal text (or skill template) and run sandbox again.
Using Sandbox to Test Skill Templates
Sandbox mode is the standard approach for validating a skill template before publishing it to a workspace or plugin. The testing protocol:
Enable sandbox on the goal
Invoke the skill with representative input values
Review the sandbox report: check that reads are pulling from the right sources and the proposed output matches expectations
Invoke the skill with edge-case inputs (empty data source, long name, special characters)
Run in sandbox with optional connectors de-authorized — verify fallback sections appear as expected
Once satisfied, toggle to live mode and run once with real inputs to confirm the live path works
Record the test inputs and outputs as documentation for the skill
Common issues found during sandbox testing
Issue
Symptom in sandbox report
Fix
Wrong Drive folder
Output written to root instead of expected subfolder
Specify folder path explicitly or use {{output_folder}} placeholder
Gmail search too broad
Engine reads hundreds of threads; reasoning is slow
Narrow the search query (date range, sender domain)
Slack post to wrong channel
Sandbox report shows post going to #general instead of #team-name
Hardcode the channel name in the template or use a placeholder
Missing fallback
Sandbox report shows empty section when connector unavailable
Add explicit fallback text for each optional connector
Output too long for Slack
Sandbox preview truncated at Slack's 4000-char limit
Summarise for Slack; write full output to Drive instead
Sandbox and Scheduled Goals

Scheduled goals can be run in sandbox mode for dry-run verification before enabling live execution on a recurring schedule. This is strongly recommended for any goal that writes to shared resources or sends external communications.
Dry-run before scheduling
Author the scheduled goal with all connector references and output instructions
Enable sandbox mode on the goal
Run the goal manually at the intended scheduled time (or simulate the data conditions it will encounter)
Review the sandbox report for all weeks/periods you want to test
Disable sandbox when the report consistently shows the expected output
Enable the schedule — the goal runs live on the configured cadence
Periodic sandbox re-runs of scheduled goals

Long-running scheduled goals can drift — data sources change structure, connector permissions expire, or downstream targets (Drive folders, Slack channels) are renamed. Running a periodic sandbox audit of scheduled goals catches these issues before they cause a silent failure in a live run.
Monthly sandbox audit: run each critical scheduled goal in sandbox and review the report
After any significant change to the connected services (Drive folder renamed, Slack workspace restructured), run a sandbox check
If the sandbox report shows unexpected reads or wrong output destinations, update the goal before the next live run
Sandbox for Team Training

Sandbox mode is the recommended environment for introducing new Cowork users to goal authoring. New users can explore what different goals do, invoke skills, and see the engine's reasoning and proposed output — all without any risk of accidentally writing to shared Drive folders, posting to Slack, or sending email.
Set workspace sandbox as default during onboarding week — new users cannot accidentally post or write
Have new users run training goals in sandbox and compare the sandbox report to the expected output
New users can author and test their own goals in sandbox before asking for review and promotion to live
Use sandbox to demonstrate a new skill template to a team before rolling it out — run it live in a meeting with the sandbox report projected
Sandbox Report Deep Dive
Understanding the sandbox report in detail helps you interpret what the engine is doing and catch subtle issues before they reach live execution.
Anatomy of a sandbox report

Example sandbox report for /sales-deal-memo (Acme Corp):

--- REASONING TRACE ---
Step 1: Searching Gmail for threads involving @acmecorp.com (last 90 days)
  Found 14 threads. Extracting: stakeholders, objections, commitments.
  Identified: John Smith (CTO), Lisa Park (Procurement), 3 objections.

Step 2: Checking Drive for existing account notes
  Searching "Accounts / Acme Corp" folder...
  Found 2 documents: "Acme Corp Kickoff Notes" (2024-01-15), "Q2 Review" (2024-06-03)
  Extracted: onboarding history, Q2 commitments.

Step 3: Assembling deal memo
  Output format: Google Doc, 4 sections.

--- INTERCEPTED WRITE ACTIONS ---
[BLOCKED - Drive] Create new document:
  Title: "Acme Corp — Deal Memo — 2025-01-20"
  Destination: Drive / Deal Memos /
  Content: [full document preview follows]

  Account Overview
  Acme Corp is a 500-person SaaS company in the logistics space...
  [continues]

--- ACTION LOG ---
10:23:01  Gmail read: query=@acmecorp.com after:2024-10-20, returned 14 threads
10:23:04  Drive read: search "Accounts / Acme Corp", returned 2 docs
10:23:07  Drive read: doc "Acme Corp Kickoff Notes" (full text)
10:23:08  Drive read: doc "Q2 Review" (full text)
10:23:12  [BLOCKED] Drive write: create "Acme Corp — Deal Memo — 2025-01-20"
10:23:12  Run complete (sandbox mode)

Reading the action log in order reveals the engine's execution sequence. If you see unexpected reads (the wrong Slack channel, a Drive folder you did not intend), the action log shows exactly what was queried and when.
Verifying read correctness
Because reads are not intercepted, the sandbox report tells you what real data the engine found. Check:
Did the Gmail search return the threads you expected? (Check result count and thread titles in the reasoning trace)
Did the Drive search find the right files? (Check document names and folder paths)
Did the Chrome connector read the right URL? (Check the URL in the action log)
Were there unexpected reads? (The action log shows every read — if the engine read files you didn't intend it to, the goal text is too broad)
When sandbox output differs from live output
Sandbox and live outputs should match for the same data conditions. Differences indicate one of three things:
Data changed between runs: Gmail threads were added, a Drive file was updated, a Slack channel got new messages — the engine read different data in each run

A write action in the live run changed what the engine could read in the next step: for example, a live run that writes a doc to Drive and then reads it back in a later step will diverge from sandbox (which never wrote the doc)
A non-deterministic engine response: rarely, the same input can produce slightly different phrasing — the core content should be stable, minor phrasing may vary
Sandbox in Multi-Step and Subagent Goals

Goals that use subagents or multi-step execution have sandbox behavior that is important to understand: sandbox applies at the top-level goal scope. Subagents spawned by the orchestrating goal also run in sandbox mode — no subagent can write to external services if the top-level goal is in sandbox mode.
Subagent sandbox behavior
Each subagent in a subagent goal inherits the sandbox setting of the parent goal
A sandbox-mode orchestrator spawns sandbox-mode subagents — none of them can write
The sandbox report for a subagent goal shows the intercepted actions from all subagents, not just the orchestrator
Subagent reads are real in sandbox mode — they read the same data they would in live mode
Testing multi-step goals in sandbox

For goals that have multiple phases (research → draft → write), sandbox mode lets you verify the first phase's output before the second phase uses it. Since the draft phase in sandbox produces a preview (not a real document), the third phase (write) will note in the sandbox report that it would have written based on the draft preview.

Example: Research → Draft → Write goal in sandbox

Sandbox report:
Step 1: Chrome connector read 6 source pages [reads executed]
Step 2: Assembled research summary [reasoning, no connector call]
Step 3: [BLOCKED] Drive write: create "Research Summary — Topic.docx"
         Preview: "Research Summary"
                  Source 1: [extracted finding 1]
                  Source 2: [extracted finding 2]
                  ...
Step 4: [BLOCKED] Gmail send: to research-team@company.com
         Subject: "Research complete — Topic"
         Body: "The research brief is ready at [Drive link]"
         Note: Drive link would reference the blocked write from Step 3

Notice that the email in step 4 references a document that was never written (because step 3 was blocked). In the live run, the Drive write happens first and the email link is real. This is a normal sandbox artifact for sequential dependency goals.
Compliance and Audit Use Cases

In compliance-sensitive organizations, sandbox mode is used not just for testing but as part of an approval workflow: every goal that touches regulated data or external communications must run in sandbox first, produce a report, and receive human approval before going live.
Sandbox-first approval workflow
User authors a goal that would write to a regulated system or send external communications
User runs the goal in sandbox mode
Sandbox report is automatically shared with the compliance reviewer (via a workspace notification or Slack connector)
Reviewer approves the goal output in the sandbox report
User receives approval, toggles to live mode, runs the goal
Live run action log is retained for audit purposes
Workspace admins can enforce sandbox-first for specific goal categories (goals that post externally, goals that reference regulated data sources)
Sandbox reports are retained in the goal history alongside the live run logs — auditors can see both the proposed output and the actual output
Goals that deviate from their sandbox report (because data changed between runs) are flagged in the audit log
Summary

Sandbox mode runs a goal fully — reasoning, connector reads, output assembly — but intercepts all write, send, and delete actions. The engine sees real data; external systems remain unchanged. The sandbox report shows the complete proposed output for review before committing to live execution.
Enable per-goal, as a personal default, or as a workspace default (admin-set)
Reads always execute; writes/sends/deletes are always intercepted
The sandbox report shows intercepted actions inline with their full proposed content
Use sandbox to validate skill templates before publishing to a workspace or plugin
Strongly recommended for dry-run testing of scheduled goals before enabling live execution
Effective for onboarding new users — they can explore and test without risk
